subject: Criminal Law
lesson_title: Sexual Offences Against Children and Protected Persons

questions.question: What is the primary legislation governing sexual offences against children in Ireland?

options.A: The Sexual Offences Act 2003 (England and Wales), applied in Ireland by statutory instrument
options.B: The Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2006, as significantly amended by the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2017
options.C: The Child Trafficking and Pornography Act 1998, which covers all sexual offences against children
options.D: The Criminal Law (Rape) Amendment Act 1990, which was extended to children by the 2017 Act

questions.explanation: The primary legislation governing sexual offences against children in Ireland is the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2006, which was significantly amended by the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2017. The 2006 Act created specific offences for sexual acts with children and established the near-age peer defence. The 2017 Act substantially updated the 2006 Act, including introducing the definition of a 'protected person' (s.21(7)) and the legislative consent definition (s.48, substituting s.9 CLRA 1990). Option A is wrong — the Sexual Offences Act 2003 is English legislation with no direct application in Ireland. Option C is wrong — the Child Trafficking and Pornography Act 1998 covers trafficking and child pornography offences, not the full spectrum of sexual offences against children. Option D is wrong — the 1990 Act was not 'extended' to children by 2017.

questions.question: As a general rule, can a child under 17 consent to sexual acts for the purposes of criminal liability of the other party?

options.A: Yes — a child who is Gillick competent can consent to sexual acts regardless of age
options.B: No — as a general rule, consent is not a defence to sexual acts with children under 17; the child's age means that the law treats any such act as criminal regardless of apparent consent, to protect children from exploitation
options.C: Yes — a child aged 14 or over can provide legally effective consent to sexual acts
options.D: No — but only for children under 15; children aged 15 and over can provide legally effective consent

questions.explanation: As a general rule under the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2006 (as amended 2017), consent is not a defence to sexual acts with children under 17. The law creates a protective framework — the child's purported consent does not provide a defence to the other party, as children are presumed to lack the full capacity and freedom from exploitation needed for meaningful consent in the criminal law sense. This is a strict liability element regarding age — the child's apparent willingness to engage does not negate liability. The rationale is protective: the law shields children from adult sexual exploitation by removing consent as a defence regardless of the child's subjective state of mind. There is one exception — the near-age peer defence under s.3(6) of the 2006 Act (discussed in subsequent questions). Options A and C incorrectly allow child consent as a defence. Option D incorrectly sets the relevant age at 15.

questions.question: Section 3(6) of the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2006 creates the 'near-age peer defence.' What are the conditions for this defence to apply?

options.A: The defence applies where both parties are under 18 and within 5 years of age of each other
options.B: The defence applies where the child was aged 15 but under 17; the accused is younger than the child or not more than 2 years older; the accused was not in a position of authority over the child; and the relationship was not intimidatory or exploitative
options.C: The defence applies where both parties are under 17 and both consent — mutual consent negates criminal liability
options.D: The defence applies where the child was under 17; the accused is under 21; and no exploitation occurred

questions.explanation: The near-age peer defence under s.3(6) Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2006 has four cumulative conditions: (1) The child must have been aged 15 but under 17 at the time — children under 15 are outside the defence; (2) The accused must be younger than the child OR not more than 2 years older — this is the narrow age proximity requirement; (3) The accused was not in a position of authority over the child — teachers, coaches, parents, guardians are excluded; (4) The relationship was not intimidatory or exploitative. All four conditions must be satisfied. The narrow age gap (2 years maximum) reflects the policy that the defence is for genuine peers — teenagers in similar life stages — not for adults targeting teenagers just under 17. Option A states an incorrect 5-year age gap. Option C incorrectly frames it as mutual consent. Option D incorrectly sets the upper age at 21.

questions.question: Section 5 of the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2006 provides that a female child under 17 shall not be guilty of an offence by reason only of engaging in sexual intercourse. What is the rationale for this provision?

options.A: The provision creates gender discrimination in favour of female children — male children under 17 remain criminally liable for engaging in sexual intercourse
options.B: The provision reflects the policy that female children under 17 who engage in sexual intercourse are victims who should be protected by the law, not criminalised; the criminal law should focus on the adult or older party who exploits the child, not on the child herself
options.C: The provision applies equally to male and female children — it simply uses 'female child' as the statutory drafting convention
options.D: The provision means that the male party in intercourse with a female under 17 also cannot be prosecuted — both parties are immunised

questions.explanation: Section 5 of the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2006 provides that a female child under 17 shall not be guilty of an offence under the Act by reason only of engaging in sexual intercourse. The rationale is protective and victim-centred: female children under 17 who engage in sexual intercourse are regarded as victims who have been exploited (or placed at risk of exploitation) by the other party. The criminal law focus is properly on the party who engages in the sexual act with the child, not on the child herself. Criminalising the child would contradict the protective purpose of the legislation and would deter child victims from reporting abuse. Option A identifies the provision correctly but frames it as discrimination rather than victim protection. Option C is incorrect — the provision expressly refers to female children only; the gendered drafting reflects the historical legislative approach to female sexual exploitation. Option D is wrong — the immunity applies to the female child only; the adult party remains liable.

questions.question: Section 21(7) of the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2017 defines a 'protected person.' What is the significance of this definition?

options.A: A 'protected person' is anyone under 18 — the definition simply restates the standard child protection threshold
options.B: A 'protected person' is a person who, due to a mental or physical condition, lacks capacity to consent to a sexual act — specifically a person who is incapable of understanding the nature or consequences of the act, evaluating relevant information to decide whether to engage, or communicating consent; specific sexual offences protect such persons regardless of their age
options.C: A 'protected person' is a person in authority over a child — the definition protects authority figures from false accusations
options.D: A 'protected person' is a person who has previously been the victim of sexual assault — it creates enhanced penalties for repeat victimisation

questions.explanation: Section 21(7) Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2017 defines a 'protected person' as a person who, due to a mental or physical condition, lacks capacity to consent to a sexual act. Specifically, a protected person is incapable of: (a) understanding the nature or reasonably foreseeable consequences of the act; OR (b) evaluating relevant information to decide whether to engage in the act; OR (c) communicating consent. This definition extends the criminal law's protection for persons who cannot meaningfully consent to sexual acts — regardless of their age — due to intellectual disability, severe mental illness, or significant physical condition. It fills the gap that would otherwise exist for adults with conditions preventing meaningful consent. Option A incorrectly limits the definition to age. Option C inverts the definition entirely. Option D creates a repeat-victim category that does not exist in the statute.

questions.question: For the offence of incest in Irish law, what specific mental element must be proven regarding the blood relationship?

options.A: The accused need not know of the blood relationship — incest is a strict liability offence as regards relationship
options.B: The accused must have knowledge of the blood relationship — recklessness as to the existence of the relationship is insufficient; the prosecution must prove the accused knew they were related to the complainant
options.C: The accused must have had constructive notice of the blood relationship — they must have taken reasonable steps to verify the relationship
options.D: The accused must have intended to commit incest — a general intent to have sexual intercourse is insufficient

questions.explanation: For the offence of incest in Irish law, the prosecution must prove that the accused had knowledge of the blood relationship between themselves and the complainant. Recklessness as to the existence of the blood relationship is insufficient — mere suspicion that the parties might be related, without actual knowledge, does not satisfy the mental element. This requirement of actual knowledge is important because without it, a person who unknowingly engaged in sexual intercourse with a blood relative would face criminal liability for incest. The knowledge requirement prevents such injustice. This should be distinguished from the honest belief defence in rape — in incest, the specific mental element regarding the relationship is knowledge, not belief. Option A incorrectly applies strict liability. Option C applies a constructive notice standard. Option D conflates the general intent to engage in intercourse with the specific knowledge of relationship.

questions.question: Which family relationships are covered by incest in Irish law, and does it extend to step-siblings?

options.A: Incest covers all family members including step-siblings and adoptive relatives
options.B: Incest under Irish law applies to blood relatives (including half-siblings) but does NOT extend to step-siblings — step-siblings are related by law, not by blood, and fall outside the incest provisions
options.C: Incest applies only to direct lineal descendants (parent-child) — siblings are not covered
options.D: The definition of incest is not codified in Irish law — it is determined on a case-by-case basis by the courts

questions.explanation: The incest provisions in Irish law cover blood relatives. Half-siblings (sharing one biological parent) are included because they share a blood relationship, even if not a full one. However, step-siblings are related by legal/social ties (through a parent's marriage or relationship), not by blood — accordingly they fall outside the incest provisions. This distinction is based on the rationale underlying incest prohibitions: the genetic risks associated with close-blood-relative reproduction and the particular family trust relationships that blood relatives have. A step-sibling relationship does not carry the same genetic considerations and may be prosecuted under other sexual offences provisions if the parties are minors or one is in a position of authority. Sexual acts with step-siblings (where both are adults) are not incest in Irish law, though they may raise social and ethical concerns. Options A and D overstate and understate the scope respectively. Option C incorrectly limits incest to the parent-child relationship.

questions.question: The Criminal Law (Incest Proceedings) Act 1995 makes a specific procedural requirement for incest trials. What is that requirement?

options.A: Incest trials must be heard by a jury of 12 with a mandatory unanimous verdict
options.B: All incest proceedings shall take place in camera (in private) — the public and media are excluded from the courtroom to protect family privacy and prevent secondary victimisation
options.C: The DPP's consent is required before incest proceedings can be initiated
options.D: Incest proceedings must be heard by a three-judge panel rather than a single judge

questions.explanation: Section 5 of the Criminal Law (Incest Proceedings) Act 1995 provides that all incest proceedings shall take place in camera — meaning the public and media are excluded from the courtroom during the hearing. This is a mandatory requirement designed to protect the privacy of the family members involved (including child victims and witnesses) and to prevent the secondary victimisation that can result from public exposure of intimate family matters. The in camera rule is consistent with the broader approach to sensitive proceedings in Irish law (such as family law and certain child proceedings). Options A and D add procedural requirements (unanimous jury, three-judge panel) that have no statutory basis for incest specifically. Option C incorrectly requires DPP consent — no such requirement exists for incest proceedings (though it does for marital rape under s.5(2) Criminal Law (Rape) Amendment Act 1990).

questions.question: A 16-year-old and a 17-year-old (who is 8 months older) engage in consensual sexual intercourse. Neither is in a position of authority over the other and the relationship is not exploitative. The 17-year-old is charged with an offence under the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2006. Can the near-age peer defence under s.3(6) succeed?

options.A: No — the near-age peer defence only applies where both parties are under 17
options.B: Yes — the 16-year-old is aged 15 but under 17; the 17-year-old is not more than 2 years older than the 16-year-old (the gap is 8 months); neither was in authority over the other; the relationship is not intimidatory or exploitative — all conditions of s.3(6) are satisfied
options.C: No — the near-age peer defence does not apply to consensual sexual intercourse; it only applies to non-penetrative acts
options.D: Yes — but only because both parties are under 18; the defence would not apply if the older party was 18

questions.explanation: Applying the four conditions of the s.3(6) near-age peer defence to these facts: (1) The 16-year-old is aged 15 but under 17 — satisfied; (2) The 17-year-old is not more than 2 years older (8 months is well within 2 years) — satisfied; (3) Neither party is in a position of authority over the other — satisfied; (4) The relationship is not intimidatory or exploitative — satisfied on the facts given. All four conditions are met — the near-age peer defence should succeed, and the 17-year-old should be acquitted. This is precisely the scenario the defence was designed for: teenagers in similar life stages engaging in consensual sexual activity without exploitation. Option A incorrectly requires both parties to be under 17. Option C incorrectly limits the defence to non-penetrative acts. Option D incorrectly ties the defence to both parties being under 18 — the conditions focus on the age gap and authority/exploitation, not on whether the accused is under 18.
